message:
security(update): pin install.sh/install.ps1 to a release tag, document trust window

`dcg update` was unconditionally piping the contents of
`raw.githubusercontent.com/.../main/install.sh` (or `install.ps1`) to
bash/PowerShell. Two problems:

1. **`main` may carry in-flight installer changes** — a refactor on
   `main` is not necessarily a tagged release. Users who run
   `dcg update` between tags can land on installer logic that is not
   the one shipped with the binary they end up with.
2. **Bare `curl | bash` of `main` is a wider supply-chain attack
   surface than necessary.** Even without a compromise, a malformed
   intermediate `main` commit could brick installs system-wide.

This commit pins the installer URL to a tag derived from either
`--version <X>` (the user's explicit target) or the current binary's
`CARGO_PKG_VERSION` env var (set by Cargo at compile time, baked into
the running binary). Tag normalization adds a leading `v` if the
caller passes a bare semver, matching the project's release-tag
convention. The resulting URL points at:

    https://raw.githubusercontent.com/.../destructive_command_guard/<tag>/install.sh

so the script that runs is the one that shipped with the requested
release, not whatever happens to be on `main`.

This is **mitigation, not elimination**: a GitHub account compromise
still lets an attacker push arbitrary code at the chosen tag, and
even at the version's tag the `curl | bash` flow trusts whatever
github.com returns for that path. Real defense requires checksum-
or sigstore-verifying `install.sh` itself before running it. The
commit prints a `WARNING:` to stderr documenting that gap and
pointing users who need stronger guarantees at the manual install
path (download a release tarball, verify, run locally).

`self_update_unix` and `self_update_windows` apply the same pinning
and warning shape; the windows path also keeps its existing
PowerShell-escaping for the constructed URL, just against the
templated string instead of the previous string-literal constant.

Tests covering the validation pipeline land alongside the existing
suite (added in the security-hardening pass earlier this session):

- `git_diff_rejects_flag_like_rev_range`: `--git-diff` rev-range
  inputs starting with `-` (`--output=...`, `--ext-diff`,
  `--upload-pack=evil`, bare `-`) must be rejected before reaching
  `git diff` to prevent argument injection.
- `git_diff_rejects_shell_metacharacters`: `;`, `&&`, `|`, `\n`,
  `$()`, backticks, NUL — defense in depth in case downstream
  callers ever stringify the value into a shell command.
- `git_diff_accepts_legitimate_rev_ranges`: the realistic set
  (`HEAD`, `HEAD~3..HEAD`, `main..feature`, `release/1.0..HEAD`,
  `v1.2.3...v2.0`, `HEAD@{1}`, abbreviated SHA, SHA..SHA) all pass
  unchanged.
- `git_diff_rejects_empty`: empty rev-range is its own failure mode.

These tests were authored as a single suite; they ship in this
commit because the same `validate_git_rev_range` function gets a new
caller from the install-URL-pinning path's test fixtures.
